Q1: I organized my study routine around blocks of hands-on practice because I really need to do things to understand them. I'd set aside about 3-4 hours most days, usually in the afternoon when I feel most alert. I kept a planner where I'd write down what I needed to work through, and I'd physically tick things off as I completed them. Having all my resources available helped me create a dedicated study space where I could spread out my notes and materials.
Q2: My pattern was somewhat regular, but it definitely shifted based on deadlines and what was coming up in my extracurriculars. I tried to keep Monday, Wednesday, and Friday afternoons consistent for coursework, but weekends would fluctuate depending on whether assignments were due. When I had multiple things happening at once, I'd compress my study time and work in longer sessions rather than shorter ones throughout the week.
Q3: Honestly, I usually logged in when I needed to check if there were new announcements or to submit something. Sometimes I'd log in to watch a specific video that I knew would help me understand a concept better. I wasn't one of those people who checked it constantly - I'd go in with a purpose, get what I needed, and then work offline with that material.
Q4: There was a period mid-semester when logging in became more routine for me, especially when the discussion boards got more active. I found myself checking in to see what my peers were saying and to add my own thoughts. It happened naturally once I realized the discussions were actually helpful and not just busywork. But I wouldn't say it ever became a daily habit like checking social media or anything.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful because I could pause, rewind, and actually work through problems alongside the instructor. I spent over 200 minutes watching them because I'd often go back to specific sections multiple times. The discussion forums were also valuable when classmates would explain things differently than the professor - sometimes hearing it another way just clicks. I'm a kinesthetic learner, so anything I could actively engage with felt more worthwhile.
Q6: I tended to skim through the supplementary readings pretty quickly or skip them entirely if I felt I understood the core material from the videos. Some of the optional quizzes I ignored because they didn't count toward my grade and I was already managing a lot with my extracurriculars. I know that sounds bad, but I had to prioritize, and my task completion reflects that I focused on what seemed most essential.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to what I'd be tested on or when it helped me actually understand something I was struggling with. If I could walk away feeling like I'd genuinely learned something or built a skill, that made it worthwhile. I also valued activities where I could interact with the material physically - taking notes, working through problems, discussing with peers.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different - there was less pressure, which was nice, but also less motivation to complete them when I was already stretched thin. Required activities had this urgency that made me prioritize them even when I was tired. With optional stuff, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, and sometimes later never came. The CBT session I did helped me recognize this pattern and be more intentional about it.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then print them out so I could annotate by hand. I had a physical notebook where I'd rewrite key concepts and work through practice problems. I also used YouTube videos and external websites when I needed a different explanation. The LMS was really just my starting point, not where all my learning happened.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be at my desk for hours working through problems in my notebook, completely offline. I'd have downloaded the materials earlier, so there was no need to be logged in. Group study sessions with classmates also happened in person or over video calls that weren't through the LMS. All that effort wouldn't show up in any log, but it was real, focused work.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never go back to it online. My video watch time might seem high, but a lot of my review happened from my own notes afterward. It was more efficient for me to have everything locally so I could study without needing internet or dealing with the LMS interface.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really difficult because I had a lot happening with my extracurriculars and several assignments due at once. I remember feeling surprised by how quickly everything piled up. My anxiety spiked during that time, and even though my stress is generally low, I felt overwhelmed. The CBT session actually helped me work through some of those feelings and develop better coping strategies.
Q13: Fatigue was probably my biggest challenge - after long days with activities and classes, I'd log into the LMS but not really engage deeply. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between doing an assignment thoroughly or just getting it done. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid the LMS temporarily because I didn't want to face material I didn't understand. I'd come back to it once I felt more capable.
Q14: There were definitely times when I'd have the LMS open but I was just going through the motions - scrolling through content without really absorbing it. This usually happened late at night when I was tired but felt like I should be doing something productive. I'd watch a video but realize at the end that I hadn't retained much because my mind was elsewhere. Those sessions felt like wasted time honestly.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts sometimes because they required more mental energy than just watching a video or reading. Even though I knew participating would help my understanding and my grade, I'd put it off until closer to the deadline. I think part of it was anxiety about saying something wrong in front of peers. The CBT work helped me recognize that this avoidance was actually making my anxiety worse, not better.
Q16: Deadlines were major drivers for me - I'd ramp up my LMS activity significantly in the days before something was due. My login frequency might look low overall, but those logins were intense and purposeful. I'd structure my week around upcoming deadlines, working backward to figure out what I needed to accomplish each day. Without deadlines, I probably would have been even less consistent with the platform.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, especially when it was specific and showed me where I went wrong. After getting assignment feedback, I'd go back to related materials to fill in my knowledge gaps. Positive feedback motivated me to keep pushing, while critical feedback, though harder to receive, helped me focus my study efforts. I appreciated when instructors took time to explain things rather than just marking points off.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules and deadlines helped me stay on track, even if I wasn't always ahead of schedule. Having 16 online courses meant I needed that structure to keep everything organized. The way materials were sequenced made sense to me and helped me build understanding progressively. However, the volume of content sometimes felt overwhelming, and I had to be strategic about what to prioritize.
Q19: The video format made engagement easier because I could learn at my own pace and revisit confusing parts. The discussion forums were set up well for peer interaction, which I appreciated. What made it harder was when there were too many small tasks that felt administrative rather than educational - those drained my motivation. Also, the interface wasn't always intuitive, which created friction when I just wanted to access content quickly.
Q20: I think students might be searching for something specific, getting confused, or trying to figure out what they actually need to do. High activity could also mean someone is struggling and repeatedly reviewing material without making progress. Or maybe they're engaging with content but having trouble translating that into completed work due to time constraints or difficulty with the assignment format. It doesn't necessarily mean they're not trying.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My login frequency was only 2, which seems low, but I passed the course and did reasonably well on assignments. I did a lot of learning offline with downloaded materials and my own notes. Someone looking at just my LMS activity might think I wasn't engaged, but I was putting in around 24 hours a week of study - just not all of it visible on the platform.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and how long they spent on videos or pages. They capture submission times and participation in discussions. But they miss the quality of engagement - whether someone was actually thinking deeply or just clicking through. They also miss all offline studying, group work, and the emotional experience of learning. A log shows behavior, not understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively thinking about the material, asking questions, and trying to connect new information to what I already know. It's not just about showing up or logging in - it's about being mentally present and invested in learning. For me, engagement also includes the hands-on work I do offline, the discussions with classmates, and the moments when something finally clicks. It's a whole experience, not just metrics.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I grew in managing my anxiety through the CBT session, or how that improvement actually helped me engage better with coursework. It wouldn't capture the peer study sessions, the late-night problem-solving in my notebook, or the satisfaction I felt when I finally understood difficult concepts. My resilience journey - learning to bounce back from setbacks - is invisible in the data but was central to my experience this semester.
